title: Cishet female archetypes: Argentina
intro: *A field guide to labels Argentines actually use for heterosexual female archetypes.* Entries are limited to terms attested in Argentine media, scholarship, dictionaries or sustained local online use. Some are imported or historical; descriptions record usage, not approval.

sections.title: Everyday and Class-Coded Labels
sections.note: Many of Argentina’s most recognizable labels for women sort people by class, taste, neighborhood, speech and perceived respectability. Several are slurs or can become slurs depending on who uses them.

items.gloss: *Mina* is long-established lunfardo for a woman; *piba* is the everyday Rioplatense word for a girl or young woman. Both can be neutral or familiar depending on tone.
a highly tone-dependent Argentine term: affectionate address among friends, mild “idiot,” or a stronger insult when delivered aggressively.
an informal way to address a woman regardless of body type; it can be friendly, neutral or impatient depending on tone.
colloquial Argentine for a woman or girl—the feminine counterpart of *chabón*. It can identify a friend, stranger or the subject of a story.
a strong Argentine insult for a foolish, inept or contemptible woman; substantially harsher than friendly *boluda*.
a familiar address or nickname that may be affectionate and unrelated to body size. Relationship and tone determine whether it is warm or insulting.
*(imported, in heavy local use)* — a jealous, controlling or conflict-heavy partner. Argentine use follows the wider Latin American internet label rather than a uniquely local meaning.

*(class-loaded)* — affluent, aspirationally affluent or conspicuously polished. *Cheta* is the shortened form of *concheta*; accounts commonly place the latter in Buenos Aires student slang of the 1970s, though its precise origin is disputed.

*(sexist in much of its use)* — a woman cast as emotionally excessive, contradictory or as attracting and then rejecting a partner. The psychiatric word became a durable everyday insult and dating stereotype; it is not a diagnosis.

a woman regarded as emotionally demanding, insistent or disproportionately invested in communication or a relationship. The label can dismiss legitimate expectations as well as genuinely overwhelming behavior.
a conventional, hypocritical or image-conscious person who hides what they really think or do. The word is not female-specific but is commonly applied to women.
absent-minded, forgetful, late or slow to respond. This is an ordinary Argentine adjective, not a fixed social class or subculture.
a capable, admirable or impressive woman; also a common form of direct praise, as in *sos una capa*.
a naïve, foolish or easily deceived woman. It is a common mild insult and the feminine of *gil*.
friendly address meaning “queen,” used to praise, reassure, patronize or soften a request. Tone can make it warm or condescending.
colloquial for a girl or young woman, from *guacha*. It can be affectionate, neutral, class-coded or hostile depending on context.
playful praise for a highly capable or energetic woman. *Maquinola* is an internet-era variant and less universal than *máquina*.
a strong insult for a selfish, cruel or contemptible woman. It is a direct character judgment rather than a sexual label.
*(vulgar)* — an immature, lazy, sexually inappropriate or exasperating woman. The literal masturbatory meaning is often secondary.
a confident, socially skilled or stylish woman; negatively, someone smug, showy or overly sure of herself.
an arrogant or self-important woman who acts above her actual status, experience or ability.
a rigid, unthinking or fanatically partisan woman. *Cabeza de termo* more directly attacks intelligence; *termo* often emphasizes blind loyalty.
a contemptible or cruel person. The noun remains grammatically masculine in the phrase *es un sorete* even when the target is a woman.
an annoying, persistent or intrusive woman who will not stop demanding attention. *Hincha-pelotas* is invariant and more emphatic.
a police officer, informer or woman who reports others to authority. *Buchona* especially means a snitch or police informant.
a boastful or fake woman who performs achievements, wealth, toughness or status she does not possess. The noun is gender-neutral in this slang use.
a woman who builds attention through exaggerated claims, promises or self-promotion without delivering substance. The label is gender-neutral.
a woman seen as causing conflict, disorder or public arguments. *Bardera* more specifically suggests provoking or escalating confrontation.
*(pejorative)* — a person who cheats, betrays or exploits others. The lunfardo-derived insult is not gender-specific.
*(pejorative)* — an unreliable, fraudulent or boastful person who promises more than they deliver. The Argentine term is not gender-specific.
a woman who delays, complicates or repeatedly changes a decision. In dating it often means someone perceived as interested but unwilling to commit to a plan.
an unfriendly, obstructive or mood-killing person: someone who refuses the plan, enforces a rule or will not join in. The Argentine slang is not gender-specific.

*(classist and often racialized)* — a woman from, or merely associated with, a *villa*. It can be a territorial self-description, but outsiders often use it to impute poverty, criminality or bad manners.

*(class-loaded; sometimes reclaimed)* — can mean a shameless or malicious woman, but also names a barrio-coded style associated with cumbia/RKT, sportswear and conspicuous beauty styling. Context determines whether it is identity, teasing or insult.
*(misogynistic sexual insult)* — Rioplatense slang for a woman branded promiscuous or equated with a prostitute. This Argentine meaning differs from the common Spanish use of *trola* for a lie.
*(class / aesthetic pejorative)* — a person, object or behavior judged vulgar, excessive or in bad taste. Applied to people, it often turns class prejudice into a judgment about aesthetics.

a young upper- or upper-middle-class woman stereotyped through private-school and Zona Norte codes, hockey, fashion and affluent consumption. The name blends *Milagros* and *Pilar*; once mainly an insult, it is also used self-consciously online.
an easygoing, friendly or fun young woman. *Copada* is ordinary Argentine praise; the phrase is descriptive rather than a rigid subculture.
a young woman identified with her neighborhood and local social ties. It is usually a positive or neutral self-description, though outsiders may attach class stereotypes to it.
a working woman, from *laburar*. As a social label it emphasizes effort, self-support and moral legitimacy through work.
a cluster of characteristically Argentine food-based insults for a foolish, inept or ridiculous woman, usually milder and more comic than *pelotuda*.
a poor, homeless or conspicuously scruffy woman; more commonly, a joking insult for someone looking neglected or badly dressed.

*(sexist, old-fashioned)* — in its feminine Argentine use, a supposedly shameless or sexually promiscuous woman; historically it could imply prostitute, unlike the broader masculine *atorrante* (“idler” or “scoundrel”).

*(classist, often racialized)* — a woman branded vulgar, low-class or badly dressed. Argentine research treats *groncha*, *grasa* and related labels as ways class hierarchy is racialized through taste.
*(class / aesthetic pejorative)* — vulgar, mass-market or in bad taste. It can describe a person, style or object and overlaps with *grasa* and *groncha*.

*(class-loaded; sometimes reclaimed)* — a young woman associated with barrio and cumbia styles, often stereotyped through fitted clothes, makeup and nightlife. The word comes from reversing *chora* (thief) and can still carry an accusation of criminality.
*(class / gender pejorative)* — a pretentious, frivolous or affected woman, often accused of copying upper-class taste without possessing it. Older than *milipili* and still current.

a gossipy, quarrelsome or conflict-stirring woman. The Argentine label comes from the *conventillo* and preserves a gendered stereotype of its female residents; it is still used as an insult well beyond that historical setting.
*(racialized class insult)* — vesre of *negra*, used to mark a woman as poor, vulgar or supposedly criminal. In-group use does not erase its discriminatory force.
*(racist expression)* — claims that “blackness” is a moral defect rather than skin color, transferring racial language onto class and character.
a woman who collects recyclable cardboard and other materials, usually as informal work. It is an occupation and poverty marker, not a personality type.
Argentine slang for a homeless, itinerant or visibly scruffy woman. Both can be descriptive but often dehumanize poverty.
an Argentine class joke for a bohemian or anti-establishment woman whose lifestyle remains protected by affluent family resources and premium private health coverage.

*(racist / classist slur)* — a label coined in 1940s Buenos Aires for darker-skinned internal migrants, especially from northern provinces, and later tied to anti-Peronist contempt for the working classes. It is not female-specific.

*(sexist appearance insult)* — Argentine slang for a woman judged extremely unattractive or badly presented. The comparison treats her as an unwanted bundle or burden; it is dated but remains widely recognizable.
a foolish, awkward or irritating woman. The insult is usually mild and comic.
an intimidating bully who acts through a group or relies on collective force; the feminine of Argentina’s durable label *patotero*.
a woman who habitually seeks unfair advantage, exploits loopholes or takes more than her share.
Julián Kartún’s comic character: a hyperbolic parody of the wealthy, insulated *milipili*, recognizable through elongated speech, consumer excess and melodramatic self-absorption.

a 2023 Argentine social-media label for a polished beige-and-linen aesthetic associated with neutral clothes, orderliness, restrained consumption and upper-class codes; often contrasted with the *milipili*.

the ironic 2023 reply to *niña lino*: a more ordinary, practical and less curated woman, jokingly associated with buying everyday clothes and household goods at Coto rather than performing quiet luxury.

sections.title: Heritage and Bynames
sections.note: Argentine bynames often turn appearance or ancestry into familiar address. Familiarity does not make them automatically harmless: several can carry racist, xenophobic or classist force.

items.gloss: a common Argentine nickname or form of address that may be affectionate and unrelated to exact skin color, but can also become racialized or classist. Relationship and tone are decisive.
a red-haired or ruddy-complexioned woman; a routine physical-trait nickname in Argentina.
literally a Galician woman but traditionally extended in Argentina to Spanish women generally; it can be neutral, affectionate or part of ethnic jokes.
a traditional Argentine byname for women of Arab, especially Syrian or Lebanese, ancestry, rooted in Ottoman-era travel documents rather than actual Turkish origin.
a traditional label for Ashkenazi Jewish women and families, reflecting migration from the Russian Empire; it can also literally mean Russian.
in Argentina, often a fair-haired woman or a rural descendant of European immigrants, especially in the central agricultural provinces; meaning varies by region.
*(xenophobic slur)* — a derogatory label for a Bolivian woman or someone perceived as Bolivian.
a shortened label for a Paraguayan woman. It can be colloquial, but frequently carries xenophobic and classist force.
an informal label for a Brazilian woman. It may be neutral or teasing, but context can make it derogatory.
a woman in a relationship with someone who has another established partner. *La otra* frames her from the primary partner’s perspective; *amante* is the more general term.
woman whose partner is unfaithful. The label humiliates the betrayed person rather than the cheater; *cornuda consciente* adds the accusation that she knows and stays.

*(misogynistic slang)* — when aimed at a woman, an insult implying sexual availability, prostitution or a transactional attachment to a wealthy or famous man. Argentine slang also gives *gato* unrelated meanings when aimed at men.
*(often pejorative)* — a woman financially supported by a partner or family member, with an implication of dependency or insufficient paid work.

*(misogynistic in much of its use)* — woman accused of choosing partners for money, access or lifestyle. *Comebilletera* is a distinctly Argentine show-business insult strongly associated with Moria Casán’s attacks on Wanda Nara.
a woman who cheats on her partner. *Gorrear* and related words are common Argentine slang for infidelity.

wife, girlfriend or aspirant of a footballer; Argentina’s established WAG label, combining glamour and fame with the sexist suspicion that the player is being pursued as a career. It was popular enough to title the 2009–2010 TV drama *Botineras*.
*(gendered blame term)* — a woman blamed for breaking up an existing couple or family, often minimizing the responsibility of the partnered man involved.

sections.title: Political and Ideological Women
sections.note: Argentine politics supplies not just opinions but accents, friendship networks, aesthetics and dating filters. The feminist *pañuelo verde*, created in Argentina in 2003, became a transnational symbol; the opposing *pañuelo celeste* grew up in response.

items.gloss: a woman identified with Peronism; *compañera* is the movement’s customary form of political address. Neither term implies one coherent ideology, but both are strong Argentine identity labels.
a woman identified with Kirchnerism or specifically with Cristina Fernández de Kirchner. *Cristinista* emphasizes personal political allegiance more than the broader *kirchnerista*.

a woman identified with Argentine libertarian politics, especially the Milei movement. In stereotype, she rejects state-centered feminism and frames progressive gender politics as coercive or bureaucratic.
a hostile contemporary label for a Kirchnerist or broadly Peronist woman, strongly associated with libertarian and anti-Kirchnerist discourse.
a left-wing woman; *zurdita* is usually dismissive or hostile. Neither identifies a specific Argentine party.
a fascist or, more loosely, authoritarian or hard-right woman. Political context distinguishes this from *facha* meaning appearance or style.
*(politically loaded)* — an anti-Peronist person. The Argentine political use dates to 1955 and was popularized by the radio comedy *La Revista Dislocada*; it is not gender-specific.

*(anti-feminist slur)* — a hostile label that caricatures a feminist as authoritarian, hateful or extreme by comparing feminism with Nazism. Argentine activists, researchers and media document it as a recurrent form of gendered online abuse.

*(misogynistic insult; sometimes reclaimed)* — literally “mare,” used to cast a woman as cruel, domineering or contemptible. In Argentine politics it became especially associated with attacks on Cristina Fernández de Kirchner and with feminist attempts to reclaim the insult.
a supporter of Mauricio Macri or the political coalition and governing style associated with him.
a dismissive label for a Peronist woman, though it can also be used jokingly or reclaimed.
a pejorative blend of *púber* and *libertaria* for a young or immature female libertarian, prominent in Milei-era political vocabulary.

woman identified with Argentina’s abortion-rights movement and the green scarf adopted by the National Campaign for Legal, Safe and Free Abortion; especially associated with the 2018–2020 mobilization cycle.
woman identified with the anti-abortion response to the green-scarf movement, often Catholic or evangelical and organized around the slogan of protecting “both lives.”

woman identified with Trotskyism or the Left and Workers’ Front. *Troska* can be dismissive or proudly reclaimed; in stereotype she is highly organized, theoretically armed and present at every march.
a neighborhood political broker who mediates access to assistance, jobs and officials while mobilizing votes or attendance; often used as shorthand for clientelism.
woman who joins a *cacerolazo* by banging cookware in protest. The term is politically and class-coded, but its meaning depends on the protest: Argentina has no single stable *cacerolera* identity.
online label for a radical feminist, often used in Argentine disputes over sex-based politics, prostitution, pornography and trans inclusion. It may be a self-description or a hostile shorthand.
a pejorative stereotype of an affluent or culturally elite progressive woman from Palermo, accused of speaking about inequality from a socially insulated position.
a party or movement activist who follows an organization’s internal line and participates in its formal structures.
political insults for a journalist or commentator accused of receiving money or government advertising in exchange for favorable coverage.
a nationalist political insult for a woman accused of serving foreign powers or interests against Argentina.
a politician or operator skilled in *la rosca*: backstage negotiation over alliances, offices, votes and candidacies.
a dismissive or joking label for a female supporter of the Radical Civic Union (UCR).
a hostile blend of *Globo* and *boluda* for a Macri/PRO supporter, especially associated with the Cambiemos era.
a self-description used by a woman distinguishing supposedly orthodox or traditional Peronism from later branches, especially Kirchnerism.
a self-description within Argentine feminism that links gender politics to labor, poverty, territorial organizing and broader social movements.
a class-coded political insult for an affluent or highly polished Kirchnerist woman, emphasizing a supposed contradiction between her lifestyle and her politics.
a supporter of a club or team; the foundational Argentine football identity from which club nicknames and rival stereotypes are built.
a Boca Juniors supporter. The old rival insult, linked to *bosta*, has been widely reclaimed as a proud self-description.
a River Plate supporter. The rival nickname is now used both as an insult and as self-identification.
a Boca Juniors supporter identified through the club’s Genoese immigrant history in La Boca.
a River Plate supporter, from the club’s expensive player signings in the early 1930s.
a superstitious woman who repeats rituals to avoid bad luck; *mufa* labels someone believed to bring it. *Anulo mufa* cancels a spoken prediction before it can jinx the outcome.
a rival insult for a supporter judged passionless, quiet or emotionally cold. Its target changes by rivalry.
a committed female admirer or defender of Lionel Messi, especially in arguments about the national team and football history.
a female admirer of Juan Román Riquelme or supporter of his football values and Boca leadership.
a female supporter of Marcelo Gallardo’s football ideas or River leadership.
a San Lorenzo supporter, from the club’s traditional association with the black cassocks of Salesian priests.
a Huracán supporter, from the club’s proximity to the former rubbish-burning grounds known as La Quema.
an Estudiantes de La Plata supporter. The once-hostile nickname is now a standard club identity.
a Gimnasia y Esgrima La Plata supporter, traditionally connected with meatpacking workers in the La Plata–Berisso area.
a spectator associated with seated stands, often stereotyped as wealthier, quieter or more critical than supporters in the *popular*.
a female follower of Carlos Bilardo’s results-first football tradition, emphasizing preparation, tactical detail and competitive advantage.
a female follower of César Luis Menotti’s football tradition, associated with attacking play, technique and an aesthetic idea of the game.
a female supporter who rejects the media dominance of Boca and River; *Bover* blends the two club names.
a woman strongly identified with CrossFit training, its community and specialized workout vocabulary.
a regular female padel player, a label with renewed visibility during Argentina’s recent padel revival.

sections.title: Music, Nightlife and Urban Tribes
sections.note: Argentina repeatedly turns musical taste into a full social type — rolingas, floggers, cumbieras and turras aren't just listeners but locally developed subcultures with their own dress, language and class coding.

items.gloss: a woman identified with cumbia as music, dance and social scene. The label is broad and can refer to performers, fans or a barrio-coded style.
a female trap artist or a woman strongly identified with trap music and its fashion. In Argentina the term is tied to the country’s large urban-music scene.
a woman who performs, follows or regularly dances to Córdoba’s cuarteto music. The term is especially meaningful within Cordoban popular culture.
a member of Argentina’s Rolling Stones-influenced *rock barrial* subculture, associated with bands such as Viejas Locas and Los Piojos and a recognizable fringe, jeans and trainers aesthetic.
a devoted follower of Patricio Rey y sus Redonditos de Ricota or the Indio Solari. The label names a major Argentine rock fandom and cultural identity.
a woman identified with tango as music, dance or culture. It is broader than *milonguera*, which more specifically implies regular participation in social milongas.
a woman identified with heavy metal music and its scene. The label is global but deeply established in Argentina.
woman identified with goth music and aesthetics; in everyday Argentine use the label is also applied loosely to almost any tattooed or black-clad woman.
member of the 2000s emo subculture, associated in Argentina with side-swept hair, eyeliner and emotional rock; often remembered alongside the local flogger-versus-cumbiero “urban tribes” era.
a performer, dancer or committed follower of Argentine folk music, often associated with peñas and regional festivals.
woman strongly identified with folk peñas, zamba, chacarera and regional festival culture; a familiar label in Argentina’s folklore scene.
a woman identified with the *bailanta* circuit of cumbia and tropical-music venues. It can describe a regular attendee, performer or the scene’s style.
a woman who dances tango and regularly participates in the social milonga circuit. Unlike *milonguita*, this remains a current, neutral identity term.

member of Argentina’s Fotolog-born 2007–2009 youth subculture, recognized by colorful skinny jeans, styled hair, posed photos and electronic dance music. Dated, but still widely understood as an era marker.
a member of a neighborhood carnival murga as dancer, drummer, singer or organizer, strongly tied to barrio and collective performance.
a female freestyle rapper, strongly associated in Argentina with public-square battles and the country’s urban-music scene.
a woman identified with punk music, politics or style. The label is global and well established in Argentine scenes.
a devoted female admirer of Luis Alberto Spinetta, often associated with close reading of his lyrics and a particular canon of Argentine rock.
a devoted female follower of Ricardo Iorio and his bands; the identity combines metal fandom with arguments over nationalism and politics.
the feminine form associated with the 2011 Wachiturros trend and its cumbia-reggaetón dance style, neon sportswear and choreographed image. It is now mainly an early-2010s reference.
an early-2010s cumbia label popularized by Las Culisueltas, whose name and performances emphasized dance and sexual self-presentation. It is dated but still recognizable.

sections.title: Work, Money and Consumption
sections.note: These labels combine ordinary work identities with Argentine stereotypes shaped by inflation, family businesses, online entrepreneurship and everyday consumption.

items.gloss: a woman registered under Argentina’s simplified self-employment tax system; a recognizable type of precarious independent worker.
a small- or medium-business owner, stereotyped both as an overburdened employer and as a boss who invokes crisis to justify poor conditions.
a woman who repeatedly launches businesses or presents herself through entrepreneurship; the label can be admiring or skeptical.
the owner’s daughter in a family business, stereotyped as holding authority through kinship rather than experience.
a political insult for a female public employee accused of collecting a salary without working. The food metaphor is gender-neutral.
a self-appointed online economic commentator who confidently explains inflation, the peso and policy without necessarily having formal expertise.
a pejorative online label for a woman who monetizes aspirational advice through courses, implying that selling the method is her only proven business.
a practitioner of ontological coaching; in Argentine satire the label often implies vague motivational language, weak credentials or *chanta* behavior.
a woman who works in or runs a *cueva*, an informal currency-exchange operation in Argentina’s parallel-dollar economy.
a woman strongly identified with drinking fernet, especially fernet with cola and its Argentine social ritual.
vesre/lunfardo-derived slang for one’s wife or long-term female partner. It can be affectionate or dismissive and often appears in male speech about domestic authority or permission.

*(classist / political pejoratives)* — a woman stereotyped as living from social-assistance plans, often accused of having children to obtain benefits. *Choriplanera* is the more openly political Argentine blend of *choripán* and *planera*.

*(classist / sexist meme)* — a young, usually working-class mother framed as both resilient and irresponsible. The expression originated outside Argentina but became a documented local insult, especially toward women assumed to receive welfare while dating or going out.

a self-description that became an Argentine social-media stereotype for a mother who treats raising sons as a distinct identity, sometimes implying indulgence, possessiveness or emotional overidentification with them. The phrase can be sincere or mocking.
an imported US internet stereotype for an entitled, demanding middle-class woman. It is understood and used in Argentina, but *Mabel* is the more locally specific counterpart.
a respectable, conventionally presented woman, usually with middle- or upper-class connotations. Depending on tone, the phrase can be sincere or ironic about status and propriety.

an Argentine internet label for an older, conventional middle-class woman associated with Facebook opinions, forwarded warnings and neighborhood complaints; roughly the local cousin of the imported *Karen* stereotype.

*(imported, current online label)* — a woman who publicly embraces traditional marriage, homemaking and gender roles, often through highly curated domestic content. Argentine media use the English term while debating whether it represents choice, branding or antifeminist reaction.

the imagined ordinary middle-class housewife whom commentator Bernardo Neustadt addressed when simplifying politics and economics. The name became an Argentine media archetype for a supposedly apolitical, intellectually underestimated audience.

sections.title: Dating-App Personas and Behaviors
sections.note: Only locally attested relationship labels remain; generic dating-app behaviors coined for the earlier version were removed.

items.gloss: a recurring sexual or romantic partner without the formal status of *novia*. *Chonga* is the less common feminine of *chongo*; *amigovia* emphasizes friendship, while *garche fijo* emphasizes regular sex.
a persuasive talker, flirt or bullshitter who relies on *chamuyo*. The label can praise verbal charm or accuse her of insincerity.
an imported digital-dating label for a woman who abruptly stops responding. The Spanish formation is in current Argentine use.

a viral Argentine label coined by streamer Martín Cirio for a woman who gets involved with partnered men, popularized in 2025 through the Wanda Nara–China Suárez dispute. It is recent, misogynistic and highly person-specific.
a regular panel contributor on Argentine television or streaming who comments on entertainment, crime, politics or relationships. The occupation has become a recognizable media persona.
an Argentine entertainment label for a woman famous largely through media exposure, scandal, relationships and repeated television appearances rather than a single profession.
a woman strongly interested in, or professionally involved with, celebrity gossip (*chimentos*). It may describe an audience member, gossip account or entertainment journalist.

an influencer whose content prominently features goods or services received through barter (*canje*) rather than cash payment. The label is common in Argentine entertainment and social-media coverage.
a mildly dismissive term for a woman with limited, recent or platform-specific fame, often through television, streaming, social media or celebrity relationships.
the leading female revue performer: dancer, comic presence and glamour figure. The role was central to twentieth-century Argentine theatre and television.
a conventionally attractive young female weather presenter, a recognizable Argentine television role that often prioritized image alongside or over meteorological expertise.

sections.title: Crime and Police-Blotter Labels
sections.note: These labels are common in Argentine crime reporting. They describe alleged methods or offenses, not inherent female personalities, and headlines can turn them into sensationalized archetypes before a conviction.

items.gloss: a thief or robber. It is a standard Argentine crime label and can also become a general political insult.
a female robber who uses a motorcycle, usually with an accomplice. The feminine form is routine in Argentine crime reporting.
a drug dealer or local intermediary in illegal trade. The label is gender-neutral; the word can also mean a corrupt arrangement.
a young female thief and class-coded media archetype associated with poor neighborhoods and cumbia villera; the label can criminalize youth by appearance or origin.

a woman who shoplifts by concealing merchandise in clothing, a bag or a specially prepared garment. Argentine police and tabloid reporting use the feminine label so routinely that it functions as a recognizable crime type.

in current Argentine crime reporting, a woman accused of seducing or meeting a man, drugging him and stealing from his home, sometimes with accomplices. The label has become strongly associated with dating-app robberies.
a criminal, rogue or streetwise delinquent; sometimes used admiringly in music and barrio identity, sometimes as a direct accusation.
lunfardo labels for a police officer. All are gender-neutral in slang and can be neutral or hostile depending on the speaker.
a female pickpocket or low-level thief who steals through stealth and dexterity; an older lunfardo label still recognizable in crime reporting.

sections.title: Imported Dating-Status Discourse
sections.note: These labels are not uniquely Argentine, but Argentine media and social networks use them in current debates about gender, money and relationships.

items.gloss: *(imported, current online label)* — a woman accused of seeking male approval by presenting herself as unlike other women or by putting them down. Argentine radio, streaming and social-media discussions use the English expression.
an imported Spanish-language dating-coach label, used in Argentine social media for a woman who presents herself through standards, appearance, emotional discipline and access to resourceful men.
post-breakup female success expressed through earnings, autonomy and visible achievement. The phrase spread in Argentina after Shakira’s 2023 line “las mujeres ya no lloran, las mujeres facturan.”

sections.title: Buenos Aires Geography as Personality
sections.note: These are attested class and neighborhood stereotypes, not neutral descriptions of everyone who lives in Palermo, Nordelta or Recoleta.

items.gloss: literally a woman from Palermo; as a stereotype, an urban upper-middle-class woman associated with fashionable cafés, therapy, astrology, design and the neighborhood’s lifestyle economy.

a woman from Nordelta, stereotyped through gated-community wealth, cars, private amenities and status policing. A 2017 viral audio about neighbors lacking “visual and moral aesthetics” became a durable reference point, renewed by debate around *Viudas Negras* in 2025.
a class stereotype of the affluent, conservative or status-conscious Buenos Aires woman, invoked through Recoleta, inherited respectability and complaints framed as standards of civilization.
a woman from the City of Buenos Aires; in national stereotype, urban, fast-talking, self-important and inclined to treat Buenos Aires as the country’s default.
a stylish or attractive woman. The common Argentine adjective can praise clothing, bearing or physical appearance.
a very attractive woman; literally a chocolate sweet and a common, usually complimentary Argentine metaphor.
a highly attractive woman, usually with an implication of vitality, confidence or strong physical presence.
*(often a put-down)* — a woman identified or reduced to suspected cosmetic surgery. The label usually carries judgment about artificiality, class or sexual display.
a visibly muscular woman with pronounced, well-developed muscles. The adjective is usually complimentary or descriptive.
the imported bows, lace and ballet-flat aesthetic as used in Argentina; local versions often mix the global trend with football shirts, mate or other national references.
woman known for posting on Argentine Twitter/X, where news, politics and celebrity conflict are treated as immediate material for jokes, factions and public argument.
a devoted female fan of anime, manga and related Japanese popular culture. The label is gender-neutral and well established in Argentina.
an imported gaming metaphor for a woman portrayed as unoriginal, scripted or incapable of independent thought.
a female user or former user of Taringa!, the Argentine platform that shaped a generation of local internet culture.
a woman who posts provocations designed to generate angry replies and attention; a Spanish formation from *bait*.
a woman passionate about cars, engines and motorsport. Both are established Argentine labels; *tuerca* has a long association with racing culture.
a motorcycle enthusiast or rider. The feminine label is established even though motorcycle culture is often male-dominated.
a woman associated with speed, street racing or track-style driving and vehicle modification.

sections.title: Fictional Reference Points
sections.note: Fictional and public women adopted as mirrors, moodboards or warnings.

items.gloss: after Quino’s *Mafalda* character: a conventional, marriage-and-motherhood-centered, status-conscious woman. Argentine usage is established enough that *susanita* can function as a common noun.
woman compared with Quino’s politically concerned, ironic and questioning girl character; the counter-image to *Susanita*, though less often used as a common noun.

sections.title: Heritage Nicknames and Show-Business Memory
sections.note: A few older labels that still circulate as nicknames or media insults, not museum-only tango taxonomy.

items.gloss: colloquial Argentine term for an Italian or Italian-descended woman. Stereotyped uses emphasize expressive speech, family intensity and food, but the literal ethnic meaning comes first.
historic revue chorus performer who sang, danced and displayed her body on stage; the show-business type that preceded the *vedette*.
